claim_chain: Multimorbidity shows significant but non-monotonic relationship with healthcare utilisation
Cognitive disorders associated with reduced outpatient service use in individuals with co-occurring conditions
Relationship between multimorbidity and healthcare use demonstrates socioeconomic gradient
Health insurance exerts buffering effect, attenuating SES-related differences
Findings challenge assumption that greater complexity necessarily results in higher recorded service use
Policy implications include need for coordinated care and expansion of health insurance coverage

typical_pattern: Explanation of mechanisms (demand-side and supply-side perspectives)
Role of health insurance as moderating factor

likely_priorities: Methodological rigor in harmonising data across multiple cohorts
Validity of cognitive disorder screening versus clinical diagnosis
Generalizability across 31 countries with different healthcare systems
Causal interpretation of associations from observational data
Measurement consistency of healthcare utilisation across cohorts
likely_attacks: Retrospective harmonisation of healthcare utilisation measures may introduce bias
Cognitive disorders identified through screening rather than clinical diagnosis may lead to misclassification
Relative differences within countries rather than absolute differences limit cross-national comparisons
Observational design limits causal inference about insurance effects
Informal care not captured may confound healthcare utilisation patterns
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly states 'A limitation of this study is that healthcare utilisation measures differed across cohorts and were harmonised retrospectively.' It also notes 'cognitive disorders in our study are identified through screening assessments rather than clinical diagnoses. Differences in screening tools across cohorts may lead to under-recognition or misclassification.' The authors acknowledge 'gradient patterns reflect relative differences within countries rather than absolute differences across nations' and discuss how 'individuals with cognitive disorders may receive much informal care from family or social networks, which is not captured by conventional measures of healthcare utilisation.' These self-identified limitations form the basis for inferring likely reviewer concerns.

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
